value: Headline: WTI clawed back above $70 to settle at $71.49 (+$1.15) as last week's WPSR confirmed a 6.1 mb crude draw to 412.1 mb — but the structural story remains Cushing, where the report showed stocks fell another 1.1 mb to 18.96 mb, leaving the hub already well below the ~25 mb operational floor. The crude pull is real, yet the broader tape is being capped by Gulf supply recovery and easing Hormuz risk, leaving the curve and cracks to do the talking. Crude & products: For the week ending June 19, 2026, the latest weekly EIA report showed crude stocks drawing 6.1 mb to 412.1 mb on high run rates and steady export pull — 4-week crude exports ran 4.93 mb/d against 5.75 mb/d imports. Refinery utilization eased 0.6 pts to 96.1% with crude inputs at 17.11 mb/d, still a strong demand signal into peak summer. Products diverged from the crude tightness: gasoline built 2.1 mb to 216.3 mb and distillate built 3.0 mb to 106.1 mb, with jet adding 1.3 mb to 46.3 mb. Total stocks were essentially flat, up just 0.3 mb to 780.8 mb — the headline crude draw masking a soft product picture. Refining margins: The 321 crack sits at $54.41, with the distillate crack the standout at $63.70 versus gasoline at $49.76 — the product builds notwithstanding, margins remain robust enough to keep refiners running hard. RBOB settled $2.887/gal (+10.0 c) and ULSD $3.2188/gal (+11.0 c), both rallying alongside crude in yesterday's settle. The distillate strength against a 3.0 mb build argues for resilient diesel pull, and supports keeping yields tilted toward the middle of the barrel. Jet cracks lag at $34.96, consistent with the 1.3 mb jet build. Curve structure: WTI prompt structure stays backwardated — M1–M2 at $0.41 and M1–M12 at $4.03 — entirely consistent with sub-floor Cushing, where thin tank bottoms keep front-month barrels bid. Brent backwardation is slightly steeper out the curve at $4.33 M1–M12 but flatter at the front, M1–M2 just $0.27. The Brent–WTI spread sits narrow at $3.51; at this level the US export arb is weak, and unless WTI cheapens to re-widen the spread, expect barrels to stay home and pressure domestic balances — a counterweight to the Cushing-driven front-end bid. Product curves remain steeply backwardated, RBOB M1–M12 at $20.11/bbl and ULSD at $21.13/bbl. News & geopolitics: The dominant theme is Gulf supply recovery: a major Saudi oil terminal roared back to life, ADNOC cut Murban to $101.48 as Hormuz tensions ease, and Qatar restored LNG output — together pushing prices back to pre-war levels per Reuters even as Cushing dwindles. That recovery is the cap on the crude draw's bullish impulse. On the bearish-for-balance side, China lifted July fuel export allowances and Middle East fuel oil exports are set to hit a four-month high, adding product length into an already-soft global picture. Bullish wildcards persist: Ukrainian strikes continue to knock out Russian refineries, tightening Russia's domestic fuel supply, while Iraq floated quitting OPEC to pump more — a quota-discipline risk worth watching. With Trump publicly singling out Exxon, Chevron, Shell and BP over gas prices, the political backdrop adds noise but little immediate fundamental shift.
